weather: Today in Edmonds: Partly cloudy morning becoming mostly sunny by afternoon. High 69°F with light N-NW winds 10-15 mph. Just a 10% chance of rain. Evening stays clear and pleasant, low around 56°F with light north winds. Perfect May weather for any outdoor plans.

local_news.findings: The Edmonds City Council unanimously adopted the city's first-ever Comprehensive Safety Action Plan on April 28, 2026. This plan identifies 11 priority traffic safety projects eligible for federal grant funding and aims to eliminate traffic fatalities and serious injuries by 2040. The council also received annual reports from the city's prosecutor and public defender offices during the same meeting.

The Edmonds City Council has adopted the city's first comprehensive Safety Action Plan, which will make the city eligible for Safe Streets and Roads For All grants. The plan aims to eliminate traffic fatalities and serious injuries by 2040, with an interim goal of a 50% reduction by 2035.

Construction on pedestrian improvements and the 88th Avenue West overlay project is scheduled to resume the week of May 4, weather permitting. The city will begin its 2025 Combined Cured-in-Place Pipe (CIPP) Rehabilitation Project on May 4 to repair aging sewer and storm pipes throughout the city.

The Washington State Court of Appeals reopened a case involving Edmonds' tree regulations, potentially reviving litigation over the city's tree mandate requirements. This follows an earlier dismissal of the case as moot after the city updated its tree code in October 2025.

The Edmonds City Council approved a $415,000 contract with Jacobs Engineering to operate the gasification system at its wastewater treatment plant, marking a potential breakthrough after three years of technical challenges. The system uses gasification to turn human waste into dried solids and was the first of its kind in the country.

career.notes: Found active high school English and History teaching positions across multiple districts within 30 miles of Edmonds. Most positions are for the 2026-27 school year starting in August/September. Salary ranges vary significantly by district and experience level, with public districts typically following state salary schedules and private schools offering competitive ranges. Several districts have multiple openings, particularly Lake Washington and Seattle Public Schools. Application processes vary - some require direct district applications while others use third-party platforms like EdJobList or ZipRecruiter.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has concluded 2025-26 auditions, but invites singers to email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join the mailing list for August 2026-27 season auditions (all voice parts, Tuesday rehearsals at Epiphany Parish Seattle). Seattle Choral Company schedules July-August 2025 auditions for their 2025-26 season (Christmas in Latvia, Bach & Handel, Chariot Jubilee) at Phinney Ridge Lutheran Church—sign-up via seattlechoralcompany.org/join-us. Choral Arts Northwest hosts a free Music Educator Sing-in on March 15, 2026 at First United Methodist Church (register by March 1). No current Pacific Lutheran University choir auditions found.
family.toddler: Sno-Isle Libraries offers weekly Toddler Storytime in May: Edmonds Library with Cami every Wednesday 9:00-9:45 am, and Lynnwood Library with Nathalie every Monday 9:30-10:00 am. Imagine Children’s Museum (Everett) has Curiosity Cove guided play for ages 1-5 and Family Engagement Night science experiments—check imaginecm.org/calendar for specific May dates. Baby Storytime at Lynnwood Library Fridays 11:00 am and Preschool Storytimes also available.

global_news.findings: President Trump has told Congress that US hostilities with Iran have "terminated" during an ongoing ceasefire, arguing he does not need congressional approval for the war under the War Powers Act. This comes as the 60-day deadline for seeking legislative approval approaches. Iran has reportedly sent a new proposal via Pakistan, but Trump stated he's "not happy" with it, describing Iran's leadership as "very confused" after the killing of top military officials. The US Treasury has warned that paying Iran for passage through the Strait of Hormuz could violate sanctions.

Russia has emerged as Syria's dominant oil supplier despite the new Western-leaning government's alignment, with oil shipments jumping 75% to about 60,000 barrels per day. This shift occurred after Iran halted supplies following Assad's fall, leaving Syria dependent on Russian oil delivered via a fleet of sanctioned vessels. The trade gives Moscow significant influence in Syria while potentially straining Damascus's relations with the EU and Washington, as Syria seeks to diversify suppliers but faces limited options due to financial constraints and years of conflict.

President Trump rejected Iran's latest proposal to end the war, saying he's "not satisfied" while blaming Iran's "fractured" leadership. The shaky three-week ceasefire appears to be holding, but both countries remain locked in a standoff over the Strait of Hormuz. Trump described having only two options: "blast the hell out of them and finish them forever" or "make a deal." Meanwhile, imprisoned Iranian Nobel laureate Narges Mohammadi was urgently hospitalized after a "catastrophic deterioration" of her health, and an explosion of leftover bombs killed 14 Revolutionary Guard members in Iran.

The UK government is facing renewed scrutiny over pro-Palestinian marches, with Justice Minister Alex Davies-Jones saying the demonstrations have been "hijacked" by people seeking to sow division. Chief Rabbi Sir Ephraim Mirvis has called for a temporary ban on the marches after two Jewish men were stabbed in what police declared a terror incident. The government's independent adviser on antisemitism warned an attack in the next six months was "inevitable" as the terrorism threat level rises.

The US is withdrawing 5,000 troops from Germany amid President Trump's escalating feud with German Chancellor Friedrich Merz. This significant reduction in US military presence in Europe comes as Trump has been critical of Germany's response to the Iran War and NATO contributions. The withdrawal represents a major shift in transatlantic relations and European security arrangements, with NATO working to understand the implications of the US decision.

intellectual_journals.findings: A sweeping ProPublica investigation reveals how President Trump has systematically dismantled the federal safeguards that prevented him from overturning the 2020 election, replacing career staff with loyalists and election-deniers ahead of the 2026 midterms. The report reconstructs a previously unreported December 2020 meeting in which Attorney General William Barr convened CISA and FBI officials to interrogate whether the 2020 vote had been hacked; after hearing that Antrim County’s vote-switching was mere human error, Barr told a deputy he was about to “kamikaze” into the White House with th [TRUNCATED]

In a luminous essay on Oliver Sacks’ posthumous collection The River of Consciousness, Maria Popova explores how the neurologist-poet fused clinical observation with philosophical wonder to illuminate the 100-millisecond gap between world and mind. Sacks’ case studies of patients whose consciousness enters “standstills” reveal perception not as passive reception but as creative coalition-building among millions of neurons—an act that “marshals all that we are and reflects us back to ourselves.” The piece traces Sacks’ argument that qualia, the subjective feel of experience, arise when neuronal [TRUNCATED]

A companion essay revisits AI pioneer Margaret Boden’s trinity of creativity—intuition, analytical evaluation, and combinatorial play—to ask whether machines can ever truly originate ideas. Boden, who died in July 2025, argued that creativity requires not just pattern recognition but the embodied reservoir of lived experience that fuels “combinatorial alchemy.” The piece warns that as we train ever-larger models on human output, we risk being conditioned to “mistake the simulacrum of life for life itself,” reducing our own aliveness to algorithms and outsourcing the very faculty that defines u [TRUNCATED]

Francine Prose reviews two new novels—Lee Clay Johnson’s Bloodline and Carl Hiaasen’s Fever Beach—that tackle the grotesque violence and corruption of contemporary America while preserving the pleasures of fiction. Johnson’s protagonist, a Tennessee grifter named Wins-a-ton Alcorn, saws off his own hand in an insurance scam he believes will restore his Confederate birthright, embodying a nation so marinated in historical delusion that self-mutilation passes for redemption. Hiaasen’s South Florida satire, meanwhile, trains its gimlet eye on toxic waste dumps and megamansions swallowing the natu [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Three recent works advance triadic-relational metaphysics: Migliorini (2025) fuses analytic philosophy of religion with trinitarian ontology to model divine relationality beyond substance metaphysics; Isabelle (2026) proposes Triadic Relational Ontology (TRO), arguing that emergence and meaning require an irreducible third “relational field” sustained by tension between poles; and the 2025 Nomos volume “Trinitarian Ontologies” collects interdisciplinary papers bridging late-antique/medieval trinitarian thought with contemporary analytic and continental projects, framing triadic ontology as a transformative philosophical paradigm.

ai_systems.findings: Three 2026 papers push autonomous research from lab demos to hardened production systems. The Mimosa framework (arXiv:2603.28986) introduces an evolving multi-agent architecture that automatically synthesizes task-specific workflows and iteratively refines them through experimental feedback, achieving 43.1% success on ScienceAgentBench. Google's MARS system (arXiv:2602.02660) employs budget-aware Monte Carlo Tree Search and reflective memory to handle computationally expensive AI research tasks, demonstrating "Aha!" moments where 63% of insights transfer across branches. Paper Circle (arXiv:2604.06170) offers an open-source multi-agent discovery pipeline that transforms papers into structured knowledge graphs for graph-aware question answering. Meanwhile, RASPRef (arXiv:2603.27008) revolutionizes prompt engineering through retrieval-augmented self-supervised refinement, optimizing prompts for reasoning models like o3 and Claude without human annotation. These systems mark the transitio [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congressional UAP momentum has reached fever pitch in May 2026. Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, chair of the House Oversight Task Force on Declassification, publicly claims she has reviewed classified evidence of "non-human origin" craft and has formally demanded 46 specific UAP videos from the Pentagon by April 14, 2026, a deadline the Department of War missed. Luna now vows to hold a press conference to reveal the footage once declassified. The Pentagon’s All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) insists it is working with the White House to release "never-before-seen" UAP information, but lawmakers accuse officials of slow-walking. President Trump’s February directive to release government files on UFOs and extraterrestrial life has activated interagency meetings to digitize and declassify decades of records. Whistleblowers warn that while older cases may be releasable, any files tied to recovered technology or reverse-engineering will likely remain hidden to protect national security sec [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Medallions, Movement, and Mamdani at MOMA PS1
newyorker.dek: The cab-driving Elcharfa brothers, Bilal and Salah, star in a new piece by the artist Kenneth Tam that showcases the hardships of their jobs, Emma Allen writes.
newyorker.text:
On Election Night, Mayor Zohran Mamdani proclaimed, from a stage, “My brother, we are in City Hall now!” The remark was directed at Richard Chow, a cabdriver from Myanmar whom Mamdani had joined in 2021 on a fifteen-day hunger strike, to demand relief for the taxi-medallion debt crisis. The crisis, in brief: the N.Y.C. Taxi and Limousine Commission sells permits (“medallions”) to operate yellow cabs; the medallion—till recently a near-million-dollar investment—had its bottom drop out, owing to predatory lending and the deregulation of rideshare services. The body of Chow’s brother, Kenny, who’d accrued enormous medallion-related debt, was found in the East River in 2018. Chow would later chauffeur Mamdani to Gracie Mansion.
The other day, the artist Kenneth Tam, forty-four, was in Queens, admiring his contribution to MOMA PS1’s “Greater New York” survey of art with ties to the metropolitan area. Title: “I’m Staying Hopeful and Strong (for Bilal and Salah).” A projected video showed the sixtysomething Lebanese taxi-driver brothers Bilal and Salaheldin Elcharfa performing interpretive movements in an empty gymnasium while intoning words they wrote about the crisis (“no retirement no retirement no retirement / scared scared scared”). Also, on the floor of the gallery: wood-beaded seat covers and other car detritus.
Tam had previously worked with nonprofessional performers in his video-art projects (Craigslist-sourced fraternity brothers and cowboys). But recruiting taxi-drivers proved difficult. He tried pitching the idea from back seats, but no luck. Then a black-car driver took him to the “J.F.K. taxi hole.”
“It’s essentially a lot where all the yellow cabs go and take their breaks,” Tam said. Rideshare drivers, too, but they have a separate zone. There’s also a space for prayer. “Ostensibly, they’re there to wait for the call to go to J.F.K., but I met guys that aren’t even drivers anymore, who go to hang out.” Tam and his producer knocked on windows and handed out flyers. Finally, he approached Bilal in the bathroom line. “I did make it clear that this was not necessarily an advocacy project: ‘I’m not an activist, but I do want to tell your story, in a different way.’ ” A way that would eventually involve dancing with metal folding chairs—but one thing at a time.
Outside the museum, Bilal was parked by a fire hydrant in his yellow RAV4. Salah was still en route from Staten Island. Of first meeting Tam, Bilal said, “He asked me, ‘Are you interested in being in a movie?’ ”
“A loose documentary,” Tam clarified.
Bilal was game. “When someone has anger inside them, you want to explore it with the outsider,” he said.
“Did you appreciate then that you would have to dance?” Tam asked.
“It was really interesting, to go for it,” Bilal said. “Falling down, reaching out, maybe looking for some hope. I was sitting in a box where I just felt like, I’m not able to get out of my situation by circling around, making all the moves—this way, that way, left, right, down, up.” He punched a fist into the air.
When things for the brothers turned grim, “we were always leaning on each other,” Bilal said. “Don’t give up. Keep going.” A friend died of a heart attack when the value of his medallion tanked.
“You’re not sick of each other?” Tam asked.
“We fight. But we never really broke up. The next day, it’s as if nothing happened. That’s our attitude.”
Salah called to say that he was stuck on the Verrazzano Bridge.
Tam said that he’d learned to drive in a suburban part of Queens—“and I distinctly remember, for my road test, I didn’t even encounter a traffic light.”
Bilal laughed. “In Lebanon,” he said, “my dad told me, ‘Just drive.’ ”
Salah was still in traffic, so Bilal decided to drive to him. Eventually, he spotted his brother and honked, yelling, “Follow that cab!”
Everyone piled into one car. Salah recounted meeting Tam: “He asked, ‘What kind of song do you like?’ ” The finished video is minimally scored, but, while shooting, Tam had the Elcharfas dance to their favorite tunes. Salah’s: Air Supply’s “Making Love Out of Nothing at All.” Bilal’s: the Scorpions’ “When the Smoke Is Going Down.”
“I’m secretly hoping Mamdani makes an appearance at PS1,” Tam said.
“He’s going to help, but he’s not going to get us back to what was before,” Salah predicted. Both brothers voted for Mamdani, though Bilal also had a laminated photo of Trump (fist raised) dangling from his rearview mirror.
The brothers’ kids recently watched Tam’s film. Bilal: “They were making fun of me—‘You’re getting old! What are you doing?’ ”
Salah: “My daughter, she’s copying me. She starts making the same movements. I said, ‘Maybe I’m going to be a movie star!’ ”
“I explained to them, ‘That’s an art project,’ ” Bilal said. “ ‘The words plus the movement, it makes a story about our situation.’ Hopefully, maybe, we educate the public.” ♦

literary_pick.title: The Sympathizer
literary_pick.author: Viet Thanh Nguyen
literary_pick.summary: A Pulitzer Prize-winning novel that redefines the Vietnam War narrative through the eyes of a half-Vietnamese, half-French communist spy. The unnamed narrator flees Saigon with the South Vietnamese army, embedding himself in the refugee community of Los Angeles while secretly reporting back to his communist handlers. This darkly comic literary thriller explores themes of identity, betrayal, and the psychological toll of living between two worlds. Critics have hailed it as "a magnificent feat of storytelling" and "destined to become a classic" that will "redefine the way we think about the Viet [TRUNCATED]

enriched_articles.title: How the Iran war affects your money and bills
enriched_articles.text: From petrol prices to mortgage rates, the US-Israel war with Iran has already had an impact on people's finances in the UK. One think tank has estimated the average working-age British household could be hundreds of pounds worse off this year as a result of the conflict. Drivers have noticed that prices at the pump have jumped since the war began, but they are now dropping. Crude oil is a key ingredient in petrol and diesel, meaning higher wholesale costs make filling up a car more expensive.

enriched_articles.title: No one can predict how the US war with Iran will unfold
enriched_articles.text: War, once unleashed, can produce all manner of unintended consequences, including some that prove uncontrollable and enduring. The US president offered a lengthy bill of indictment against Iran's Islamic Republic, stretching back to the 1979 revolution: the takeover of the American embassy in Tehran, support for terrorism, brutality towards its citizenry, and support for proxies that have killed Americans. Above all, Trump stressed the peril the US and its allies would face were Iran to build nu

enriched_articles.title: The Music of What Happens: Poems of Seamus Heaney
enriched_articles.text: Seamus Heaney, a renowned Anglophone poet, was born in 1939 in County Derry, Ireland, and passed away in Dublin in 2013. His first collection, Death of a Naturalist, was published by Faber and Faber in 1966 and followed by eleven other volumes of poetry, including collections of literary criticism, anthologies, translations, and verse plays. His literary criticism and writing influenced his work significantly, with his work growing under the attention of the academy and a larger and not-yet-balk

enriched_articles.title: Quoting the World
enriched_articles.text: Eugène Atget, a former actor and actor, has been featured in a new exhibition at the International Center of Photography in Paris. The exhibition includes fifty photographs of Atget's familiar images of Paris, which are often obscured by their descriptions or narratives. During his lifetime, Atget registered with the state as an artiste dramatique primarily for tax purposes, but he never showed in a gallery or museum.

enriched_articles.title: Vengeance Is Theirs
enriched_articles.text: The brutal and bloody Titus Andronicus has been performed in a Red Bull Theater production in New York. The play, often overlooked due to its high level of violence and themes of consumption, exposes a disordered Roman state. The most notable modern version, directed by Peter Brook in 1955 and starring Laurence Olivier, caused twenty faintings at a single performance.
